content:
Ideas of **average outcome** and **spread** accompanied early probability on games and annuities in the **17th century** (**Huygens**, **Pascal**, **Fermat**), where expected winnings were computed from discrete equally likely cases.

**Bernoulli** and **De Moivre** developed generating functions and limit theorems that made expectations of sums tractable.

In the 18th–19th centuries, **Laplace** and **Gauss** tied the **arithmetic mean** to measurement error and least-squares fitting—the mean minimizes squared deviation, linking expectation to variance-type criteria.

Formal **linearity of expectation** and **variance formulas** matured with **Chebyshev**, **Markov**, and the measure-theoretic framework of **Kolmogorov** (1933), where E[X] is an integral against a probability measure.

**Fisher** and modern statistics formalized unbiased sample variance (divisor n−1) and connected moments to estimation theory.

In ML, **MSE loss**, **bias–variance decomposition**, and **stochastic gradient** estimators operationalize expectation and variance at scale—every mini-batch mean is a Monte Carlo estimate of an expected loss.

timeline.label: Classical expectation
timeline.description: Huygens and Bernoulli compute expected values for games and sums of discrete trials.

timeline.label: Least squares and the mean
timeline.description: Gauss shows the sample mean minimizes squared error—tying expectation to variance criteria.

timeline.label: Measure-theoretic formalization
timeline.description: Kolmogorov defines E[X] as an integral; linearity and variance identities follow from axioms.

timeline.label: ML and stochastic optimization
timeline.description: MSE, mini-batch gradients, and bias–variance analysis make expectation and variance central to training and evaluation.
